title: Understanding and Managing Dog Resource Guarding with Toys
meta_desc: Is your dog guarding their toys? Learn about resource guarding, urgency levels, and how to safely manage this behavior to keep your family safe.
content_html:
TL;DR: Resource guarding is a defensive behavior over food or toys that poses a safety risk; immediate professional intervention is required if biting occurs or children are in the home.

What is resource guarding and what are the common signs in dogs?

Resource guarding is a natural but often problematic behavior where a dog becomes defensive in order to keep others away from something they perceive as valuable, such as a favorite squeaky toy or a chew bone. Common signs include:

  • Body stiffening or hovering over the object
  • Growling or baring teeth
  • Snapping or lunging

When is resource guarding considered an emergency for my dog?

  • The urgency level for resource guarding is considered Medium.
  • While it is not a life-threatening medical emergency, it is a significant safety risk for the household.
  • If your dog has escalated to biting or if there are children in the home, you should contact a veterinary behaviorist or a professional trainer immediately to prevent injuries.

How does sharing a photo or video help a professional triage my dog's behavior?

Providing a photo or a short video of your dog's body language (taken from a safe distance) is extremely helpful for a veterinary professional or trainer because it allows them to:

  • Observe specific cues such as the position of the ears and the tension in the muzzle.
  • Identify 'whale eye' (showing the whites of the eyes).
  • Determine the severity of the guarding behavior.
  • Guide the creation of a safe behavior modification plan.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In adult dogs exhibiting resource guarding, management involves preventing access to guarded items or confining the dog when providing such items. Training should focus on teaching "give" and "drop" cues, starting with low-value items and high-value rewards. Trading the object for a higher-value item is an option if safety is a concern. Providing more toys and multiple small meals may reduce the perceived value and novelty of individual resources. Avoid removing food bowls, toys, or chews by confrontation, as this can increase anxiety and aggression.

Chapter: Behavioral

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1563)

faqs.question: Should I take the toy away to show my dog who is boss?
faqs.answer: No, forcibly removing the toy often worsens the behavior by confirming the dog's fear that their prize will be stolen. This can lead to increased aggression.

faqs.question: Is growling a bad sign?
faqs.answer: While it sounds scary, growling is actually a vital communication tool. It is your dog's way of saying they are uncomfortable. Punishing a growl can lead to a dog that bites without warning.

faqs.question: Can resource guarding be fixed?
faqs.answer: It can be managed and improved significantly through positive reinforcement, management strategies, and 'trading up' exercises, though it requires consistency and patience.
